message.content:
You are a DIRTY-ROOM integrator for the tamarin-rs relicensing campaign, working in /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs. Read /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/PROTOCOL.md and the relevant INTEGRATION_REPORT.md sections (the wave-2 sections document each blocker this round aimed to close).
RULES: adapters and extraction only — NEVER transplant ported logic into clean (headerless) files; clean vendored sources get mechanical fixes only. A clean file acquiring a GPL header from scripts/gen_license_headers.py is a provenance violation: stop and report. If a gap still blocks, KEEP the ported path + report precisely. Comments describe current code only. No git commits. Append a dated section to INTEGRATION_REPORT.md; return a plain-text summary.
FINAL VALIDATION: cargo build --workspace; cargo test -p tamarin-parser -p tamarin-theory -p tamarin-prover -p tamarin-server; wellformedness fixture suite; scripts/gen_license_headers.py then --check (0 stale). Report the header-count delta and author citations that disappeared. If you must stop partway, leave the tree BUILDING AND GREEN and report the precise remaining steps.

Integrate units B (graph) and A (web) in crates/tamarin-server. Audits passed this round for: E, B, A, D — only integrate passed units. An E/D integrator ran before you; rebase on the CURRENT tree.
- B (if passed): re-sync graph_clean from the round-7 workspace (budget function refined beyond flat-sum). Rebuild the live byte gate from the wave-2 round-5/6 INTEGRATION_REPORT sections (reference server, PATH /home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin, ports 3200-3299, equals-form --port=; the Wide-rule probe at interactive-graph-def/cases/raw/1/1 whose Big-cell divergence was the last failure) + fresh captures across clustered/wide/induction/compressed variants; drive the actual RawRule seam (ported repr/simplify content + ported term printer cells into clean generate). Require byte-equality. When green: route system_to_dot/_with/render_svg_or_dot_with through clean generate, update routes_graph::dot_output_for_a_simple_system to the reference dialect, DELETE the ported serialization in handlers/dot.rs and graph/abbreviation.rs if the clean abbreviation engine now serves the route end-to-end; keep repr.rs/simplify.rs ported; keep-and-report residue.
- A (if passed): re-sync web_clean from the round-6 workspace — it now has (1) origin-aware page shells and (2) the state-delegation trait (Server no longer owns the version map; it drives state through a trait). This removes wave-2 blockers A2 and A3 as clean-side gaps. Implement the state trait over the ported state::TheoryStore (async bridge adapter: the trait impl may block_on/spawn_blocking internally — extraction and bridging of ported code is allowed and stays headered), wire ProverOps over the already-pure ported producers (theory_html::{overview_page,path_html,proof_html}, render_theory_source, title_for, next/prev_theory_path, root::render_index), and route ALL routes through web_clean dispatch with axum as transport glue. Gates: every route test + captured-HS parity fixture green (routes_stubs del_path/verify included). When the ported routing/dispatch surfaces (routes.rs route table, handler dispatch shells, state.rs version-allocation logic now expressed by the clean contract) are no longer referenced, DELETE them and rename web_clean -> web. Expect a header drop; report exactly which files/authors. If the async bridge proves behaviorally unsafe (deadlock/serialization concerns under concurrent proof search), stop, keep ported, and report the precise design conflict instead of forcing.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: byte-parity corpus must pass.
43	4. Integration: dirty room may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
44	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
45	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
46	   with them (history retains them under GPL).
47

1	//! [`RenderSystem`] — a write-sealed, display-only wrapper around a prover
2	//! [`System`].
3	//!
4	//! The interactive graph-render pipeline (`graph::simplify`,
5	//! `handlers::dot`) works on a **clone** of a prover `System` that it mutates
6	//! for display: it drops entailed order constraints, hides intruder/coerce
7	//! nodes, and transitively reduces the `less` relation.  Those mutations go
8	//! through `content_mut()`, which leaves the system's verified-identity
9	//! `subst_system` stamps meaningless — so a mutated display copy must NEVER be
10	//! fed back into the prover.
11	//!
12	//! `RenderSystem` makes that unrepresentable at the type level: it is
13	//! constructed **only** by [`RenderSystem::from_prover`] (a one-way door from a
14	//! prover `System`), and it exposes the inner `System` **only** by shared/unique
15	//! reference through `Deref`/`DerefMut` — there is deliberately **no**
16	//! `into_inner`, and no accessor hands the inner `System` back by value.  So a
17	//! `RenderSystem` cannot be passed to any prover entry point that consumes a
18	//! `System` by value; the render pipeline is typed with `RenderSystem` from the
19	//! clone-for-render boundary onwards.
20	//!
21	//! Reads (`rs.nodes`, `rs.less_atoms`, …) and the display-only mutators
22	//! (`rs.content_mut()`, `rs.goals_mut()`, `rs.nodes_mut()`, …) keep working
23	//! unchanged via the deref coercions, and any function taking `&System`
24	//! (e.g. `compute_basic_graph_repr`) accepts `&RenderSystem` directly.
25	
26	use tamarin_theory::constraint::system::System;
27	
28	/// A prover [`System`] clone dedicated to graph rendering.  See the module
29	/// docs: constructed one-way via [`RenderSystem::from_prover`], never yields
30	/// its inner `System` by value, so it cannot re-enter the prover.
31	pub struct RenderSystem(System);
32	
33	impl RenderSystem {
34	    /// The ONLY constructor: wrap a (cloned) prover `System` for display
35	    /// mutation.  One-way — there is no inverse that returns the inner
36	    /// `System` by value.
37	    #[inline]
38	    pub fn from_prover(sys: System) -> Self {
39	        RenderSystem(sys)
40	    }
41	}
42	
43	impl std::ops::Deref for RenderSystem {
44	    type Target = System;
45	    #[inline]
46	    fn deref(&self) -> &System {
47	        &self.0
48	    }
49	}
50	
51	impl std::ops::DerefMut for RenderSystem {
52	    #[inline]
53	    fn deref_mut(&mut self) -> &mut System {
54	        &mut self.0
55	    }
56	}

1	//! `web-clean` — a clean-room reimplementation of the response bodies produced
2	//! by the tamarin-prover interactive web UI.
3	//!
4	//! This crate reproduces the *web layer's* output: the URL route grammar, the
5	//! JSON response envelopes, and the HTML/DOT/text page templates. Content that
6	//! the prover produces (pretty-printed terms, constraint systems, proof-method
7	//! names, graph bodies) is treated as opaque input supplied by the caller — the
8	//! crate reproduces the scaffolding, escaping, links and envelopes around it.
9	//!
10	//! Everything here was derived from black-box observation of captured responses
11	//! and live probing; see `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md` for the observed spec.
12	//!
13	//! Module map:
14	//! * [`route`] — parse a request path into a structured route (the grammar),
15	//!   plus the top-level ([`route::Toplevel`]) surface.
16	//! * [`dispatch`] — the UI state machine: route dispatch, theory-version
17	//!   management, and response assembly over the whole surface, via a `ProverOps`
18	//!   trait.
19	//! * [`envelope`] — the three JSON response shapes (`{html,title}`, `{redirect}`,
20	//!   `{alert}`).
21	//! * [`escape`] — HTML entity escaping.
22	//! * [`page`] — the theory-view HTML shell (`overview/*`, trace and diff) and the
23	//!   index page (`/`).
24	//! * [`proofscript`] — the proof-script (west) pane and proof-tree line grammar.
25	//! * [`forms`] — the edit / delete / add-lemma form bodies.
26	//! * [`intdot`] — the `intdot` mini-page and empty-graph DOT skeleton.
27	//! * [`text`] — the plain-text bodies (`source`/`message`, `next`/`prev`).
28	//! * [`assets`] — static content-type mapping and the fixed small text bodies
29	//!   (`robots.txt`, `/kill`, favicon target).
30	//! * [`errors`] — the 404 / 400 / 405 status pages.
31	
32	pub mod assets;
33	pub mod dispatch;
34	pub mod envelope;
35	pub mod errors;
36	pub mod escape;
37	pub mod forms;
38	pub mod intdot;
39	pub mod page;
40	pub mod proofscript;
41	pub mod route;
42	pub mod text;
43	
44	mod shell_template;

14	//! `/thy/trace/<idx>/main/...`.
15	//!
16	//! We keep both the parser AST and the elaborated typed theory.  The
17	//! parser AST is needed by `prove_lemma`; the elaborated theory is
18	//! used for accessor helpers (lemma list, restriction count, …).
19	//!
20	//! Concurrency: `parking_lot::Mutex` — interactive single-user UI, no
21	//! need for an async lock.  Only the autoprover (`autoprove` /
22	//! `autoprove_all`) offloads its work onto `tokio::task::spawn_blocking`;
23	//! interactive single-step applies and proof-state materialization run
24	//! inline on the async handler thread.
25

28	use std::path::PathBuf;

38	/// One loaded theory with bookkeeping.
39	#[derive(Clone)]
40	pub struct TheoryEntry {
41	    /// Stable index used in URLs.  Set by `TheoryStore::insert`.
42	    pub idx: usize,
43	    /// Theory name from the `.spthy` source.
44	    pub name: String,
45	    /// Parser AST — kept verbatim so `prove_lemma` has the same shape
46	    /// it was elaborated from.
47	    pub parser_theory: Arc<p::Theory>,
48	    /// Elaborated, typed theory — used for accessor helpers.  Wrapped
49	    /// in `Arc` so we can clone the entry cheaply.
50	    pub typed_theory: Arc<TypedTheory>,
51	    /// Where the theory came from.
52	    pub origin: TheoryOrigin,
53	    /// Load time for the UI.
54	    pub loaded_at: DateTime<Local>,
55	    /// True for the originally loaded copy (vs. ones produced by edits).
56	    pub primary: bool,
57	    /// The theory's wellformedness report, computed at load time by the
58	    /// same pipeline `--prove` runs (`theory_io::load_from_source`, mirroring
59	    /// `run.rs`'s `checkWellformedness`).  This is the single source of truth
60	    /// for both wellformedness renderings: the `/* WARNING: ... */` comment in
61	    /// the `source`/`message` routes (`format_wf_block`) and the
62	    /// `<div class="wf-warning">` header banner in the `help`/`overview` routes
63	    /// (`errors_html`).  Empty ⇒ no warnings (theory is well-formed).
64	    pub wf_report: Vec<tamarin_parser::wf::WfError>,
65	    /// HTML for the wellformedness warning banner shown in the theory
66	    /// page header (HS `errorsHtml`, rendered raw via
67	    /// `preEscapedToMarkup info.errorsHtml` at `src/Web/Theory.hs`).
68	    /// Populated from [`wf_report`](Self::wf_report) at load time,
69	    /// mirroring HS `makeWfErrorsHtml` (`src/Web/Handler.hs`), which wraps
70	    /// `renderHtmlDoc (htmlDoc $ prettyWfErrorReport report)` of the
71	    /// *closed* theory's wellformedness report in a `<div class="wf-warning">`.
72	    /// Empty string when the report is empty (HS `makeWfErrorsHtml [] = ""`).
73	    pub errors_html: String,
74	    /// Live proof state — built lazily on first request that needs it
75	    /// (theory load → only kept-around-but-empty until `ensure_proof_state`
76	    /// is asked for).  `None` here means "not yet built"; on first
77	    /// access we boot Maude and precompute the per-lemma initial
78	    /// systems.  Building this eagerly at load time would cost ~1s
79	    /// per theory for Maude startup + source precompute, which is
80	    /// fine but pushes start-of-server latency.
81	    pub proof_state: Option<Arc<ProofState>>,
82	}
83	
84	#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
85	pub enum TheoryOrigin {
86	    /// Loaded from a path on disk.
87	    Local(PathBuf),
88	    /// Uploaded via POST `/`.
89	    Upload(String),
90	    /// Generated interactively (e.g. by an edit). Currently never
91	    /// constructed — placeholder for the unported interactive-edit
92	    /// path (HS `Interactive`).
93	    Interactive,
94	}
95	
96	impl TheoryOrigin {
97	    pub fn label(&self) -> String {
98	        match self {
99	            TheoryOrigin::Local(p) => p.display().to_string(),
100	            TheoryOrigin::Upload(n) => n.clone(),
101	            TheoryOrigin::Interactive => "(interactively created)".into(),
102	        }
103	    }
104	}
105	
106	#[derive(Default, Clone)]
107	pub struct TheoryStore {
108	    inner: Arc<Mutex<TheoryStoreInner>>,
109	}
110	
111	#[derive(Default)]
112	struct TheoryStoreInner {
113	    by_idx: BTreeMap<usize, TheoryEntry>,
114	}
115	
116	/// Next free store index: Haskell's `M.findMax + 1` (empty → 1).  Single
117	/// spelling shared by `insert` and `clone_at_new_idx_with` so they cannot
118	/// drift; `next_back()` is O(log n) (unlike `keys().last()`, which walks).
119	fn next_free_idx(inner: &TheoryStoreInner) -> usize {
120	    inner.by_idx.keys().next_back().map_or(1, |k| k + 1)
121	}
122	
123	impl TheoryStore {
124	    /// Insert a new theory and return the freshly assigned index.
125	    pub fn insert(&self, mut entry: TheoryEntry) -> usize {
126	        let mut inner = self.inner.lock();
127	        // Match Haskell's `M.findMax + 1` (BTreeMap max key); empty → 1.
128	        let idx = next_free_idx(&inner);
129	        entry.idx = idx;
130	        inner.by_idx.insert(idx, entry);
131	        idx
132	    }
133

concurrent-execution model with a globally-serialized synchronous one, blocked by a
conflict intrinsic to the clean `Server` contract (evidenced from the code):

1. **`Server::dispatch(&mut self)` is SYNCHRONOUS and single-owner**
   (`web_clean/dispatch.rs`). It holds `&mut self` for a request's whole duration —
   including a multi-second autoprove — because it drives version state (`StateOps`)
   and mutates it inline. Served from axum's async multi-threaded runtime it must
   live behind ONE global lock (it owns the version state via `StateOps`), so every
   request acquires that lock for its full duration.

2. **The ported design deliberately AVOIDS exactly this** (`state.rs:20-24`:
   "interactive single-user UI … only the autoprover offloads onto
   `tokio::task::spawn_blocking`"). `handlers/theory.rs:596-608` runs the search on a
   blocking thread WITHOUT holding a global lock — the store `parking_lot::Mutex` is
   released across the search and the `ProofState` is mutated through an `Arc`
   (`graft_at_path`). So a graph fetch / index view / different-theory request
   proceeds DURING a running autoprove. Under a global `Mutex<Server>` held across
   `dispatch`, all of them block until the search finishes — the exact
   **"serialization concerns under concurrent proof search"** the task names as a stop
   trigger. It is not a fixable adapter detail: it is the clean synchronous
   `&mut self` `Server` contract. The route tests (sequential request/response) would
   NOT catch it — a silent runtime regression, which the campaign precedent
   (round-6 A2) says must not be forced.

   &Self::Theory, …)` takes the theory IMMUTABLY, but the ported proof view lazily
   materialises + caches a Maude `ProofState` into the store entry

   double-checked-locks). Under `&Theory` the caching needs the ported `TheoryEntry`
   wrapped in interior mutability (`OnceLock`/`Mutex`) — a structural change to the
   ported state type.

4. **`StateOps::get(&self, index) -> Option<&Self::Theory>` returns a borrow**,
